title: THE SERPENT STRIKES AGAIN: BULGARI'S SERPENTI THROUGH THE DECADES
description: From Elizabeth Taylor's wrist to the spring runways — how one serpent-coiled bracelet-watch became fashion's most enduring obsession.
content:
The snake has fascinated humanity since the beginning of recorded time — a symbol of transformation, of danger, of eternal return. It is fitting, then, that Bulgari chose the serpent as the animating spirit of what would become one of the most recognisable jewellery-watches ever created.

The Serpenti was born in Rome in the 1940s, conceived as a flexible bracelet-watch that coiled around the wrist with a liquid, organic grace that no conventional timepiece could replicate. Elizabeth Taylor was among the first to understand its power. Photographed wearing her Serpenti during the filming of Cleopatra — itself a story of serpents and seduction — she gave the piece a mythology it has never shed.

Decades passed. Fashion shifted. The Serpenti adapted. Through the excess of the 1980s, it grew larger and bolder. Through the minimalism of the 1990s, it retreated into refinement. Each era got the Serpenti it deserved, and the piece survived all of them.

The current collection — shown at the spring ready-to-wear shows on the wrists of models moving through a set designed to evoke the Roman Forum — represents perhaps the most sophisticated iteration yet. The serpent's scales are now rendered in hand-painted enamel, each one individually applied, a process that requires artisans to invest more than forty hours per piece. The eyes are set with precisely-cut rubies that catch the light at every angle.

Some objects transcend function and become, instead, a kind of language. The Serpenti is one of them. To wear it is to say something — about power, about history, about the pleasure of beauty for its own sake.
keywords: Bulgari, Serpenti, watches, jewellery, bracelet watch, Italian luxury, Elizabeth Taylor
